text: The surgeon defines the anatomy of the fistula, confirming a transphincteric fistula involving fifty percent of the sphincter complex, with no visible signs of active sepsis.
The surgeon uses electrocautery to make a circumferential incision around the internal opening of the fistula. Following this, sharp dissection is employed to excise the internal opening.
The surgeon raises flaps of mucosa and a small cuff of internal sphincter above and below the internal opening, ensuring haemostasis and irrigating the wound while avoiding excess charring.
The surgeon closes the internal opening of the fistula using a series of interrupted polysorb sutures, effectively sealing the defect.
The surgeon removes the Hill Ferguson retractor and sequentially ties the sutures that were previously placed to close the defect at the internal opening.
The retractor is reinserted into the surgical site, and the sutures are cut. The surgeon then opposes the edges of the flap, aligning them precisely.

The surgeon places a series of interrupted tuopolysorb sutures to oppose the flap edges, taking care to avoid excess tension. Generous bites of tissue are taken to ensure good apposition between the anoderm and rectal mucosa, minimizing dead space beneath the flap.
The surgeon removes the retractor and sequentially ties the sutures, ensuring the flap edges are securely opposed.

The surgeon reinserts the retractor and performs irrigation to clear the surgical field. Visible defects in the flap are identified, and additional sutures are placed to ensure proper closure and apposition.
The surgeon displays the external fistula opening, ensuring it is visible to prevent recurrence of an abscess or fistula.
